title: Syllabus
text: Dynamic Corporate Transformation (DCT) develops the enterprise as a dynamic system and enterprise transformation as a mathematical optimization problem. Weeks 1–7 build the framework (Volume I) and culminate in the General Enterprise Optimization Problem; weeks 8–14 solve it (Volume II) across convex, nonlinear, dynamic, stochastic, robust, multi-objective, and AI-assisted formulations, closing with digital twins and integrated case studies.

title: Syllabus

title: Syllabus
text:
Weekly rhythm
Read the chapters before class; lecture follows the chapter’s section flow; the lab session runs the chapter’s AXIOM modules; the problem set draws from the exercise groups, with starred (★) exercises for the advanced track. Full solutions are in the Instructor’s Manual — instructors, see Instructor Resources.

title: 14-Week Schedule
text:
The course runs Volume I in weeks 1–7 (posing the General Enterprise Optimization Problem) and Volume II in weeks 8–14 (solving it). The double rule marks the volume boundary: the midterm sits exactly where the GEOP is posed. Instructors may re-balance — every chapter page is self-contained.

title: The enterprise as a dynamic system. Transformation as an optimization problem.
text:
The four-part teaching system
The book is the controlling document of a chapter-aligned ecosystem: the two-volume text, the Instructor’s Manuals, this course website, and the AXIOM computational laboratory. Every chapter page carries its Learning Outcome Statements, a reading guide, an exercise index, its AXIOM modules, and three downloadable companions — a lecture deck, a seeded Python notebook, and a seeded Excel workbook that agree by construction.

title: Chapter 16 · Enterprise Applications and Integrated Transformation Case Studies
text:
Exercises
12 exercises, grouped A concept checks · B mathematical · C computational · D enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the advanced track. Full solutions appear in the Instructor’s Manual, Chapter 16.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 16.1 — For each case study, name the binding structure, its dual price’s managerial meaning, and the convention the optimized answer displaced.
Exercise 16.2 — The energy case found expectation and worst case agreeing; construct a plausible parameter change (capex, carbon distribution) under which they disagree, and explain which Chapter 9–11 doctrine then governs the build.


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 16.3 — Derive the water-filling solution of the government case from the KKT conditions, including the threshold condition under which an area receives zero, and verify \((28.5, 18.0, 3.5, 0)\) analytically.
Exercise 16.4 — Derive \(t^{*} = (g - \rho)/d\) for the private-equity case, extend to a stochastic \(g\) learned by the NAV twin (replace \(g\) by the posterior mean), and quantify …


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 16.5 — Prove that in the bank LP any optimum exhausts the risk budget whenever every line’s return is positive, and that the optimal allocation is monotone in the risk budget (parametric LP argument); interpret both facts for capital committees.


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 16.6 — Reproduce the manufacturing case; then sweep the ESG intensity ceiling from \(80\) to \(110\) and plot NPV and \(g^{*}\) against it, locating the ceiling at which green investment becomes voluntary.
Exercise 16.7 — Reproduce the technology portfolio; then let an agentic scanner (Ch.
Exercise 16.8 — Prepare the healthcare case’s executive presentation: one page of formulation in clinical language, the computed mix with the binding ratio explained, the twin’s resilience numbers, and the three questions a chief medical officer will ask with your answers.
Exercise 16.9 — Design the government case’s public disclosure: publish the welfare model, weights, saturations, and KKT price in a form a legislature can debate, and defend the program-4 corner against the equity objection.
Exercise 16.10 ★ — Formulate the seven cases as a federated ecosystem (Definition 15.
Exercise 16.11 — Full-stack engagement: select any real or synthetic enterprise, execute the complete Figure (see book) workflow—architectures, GEOP, learning, AI augmentation, twin, roadmap—and deliver the executive pack plus the AXIOM-16 laboratory instance, with every headline number computed and every binding structure named.
Exercise 16.12 — Post-transformation audit: for a completed transformation (yours from Exercise (see book) or a published case), reconstruct what the DCT stack would have recommended, measure realized against recommended decisions, attribute the gap across the Theorem 15.

title: Chapter 15 · Enterprise Digital Twins and Autonomous Enterprise Transformation
text:
Exercises
12 exercises, grouped A concept checks · B mathematical · C computational · D enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the advanced track. Full solutions appear in the Instructor’s Manual, Chapter 15.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 15.1 — For each row of Table (see book), explain what breaks if an asset-twin practice is transplanted unmodified to the enterprise twin, and which section of this chapter supplies the repair.
Exercise 15.2 — Draft the graduated-autonomy protocol of (see book) for the treasury loop of Example (see book): the evidence thresholds (stability margins, coverage, false alerts, regret history) that expand the perimeter, and the breaches that contract it.


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 15.3 — Derive the fusion identity of (see book) from the Kalman update with stacked observations, and quantify the precision overstatement when two “independent” sensors share a common upstream source with correlation \(\rho\).
Exercise 15.4 — Verify the scalar Riccati fixed point of Example (see book) analytically (solve \(\phi(P) = P\)), confirm \(P_{\infty} = 0.0695\) and \(K = 0.278\), and compute the sensitivity \(\partial P_{\infty} / \partial r\)—the value of a better sensor.
Exercise 15.5 — Extend Theorem (see book) to time-varying gains \(K_t \to K\): show the error dynamics remain geometrically stable once \(\rho(A - AK_tC) \le \bar{\rho} < 1\) for all large \(t\).
Exercise 15.6 — In Proposition (see book)’s setting, derive the optimal confidence \(z^{*}\) trading false-alert cost \(c_{\mathrm{FA}}\) against per-period breach cost \(c_{\mathrm{B}}\), and evaluate it on the bank instance of Example (see book).


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 15.7 — Prove the multivariable extension of Theorem (see book)(iii): under detectability of \((A, C)\) and stabilizability of \((A, Q^{1/2})\), the Riccati iteration converges to the unique positive-semidefinite stabilizing fixed point (follow the monotone-operator route; cite Volume I where needed).
Exercise 15.8 — Prove that Theorem (see book) fails without separation structure: exhibit a nonlinear (or multiplicative-noise) loop where estimation and control are each stable in isolation but the interconnection is unstable, and identify which proof step breaks.


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 15.9 — Reproduce Example (see book); then sweep the manual cadence from \(1\) to \(8\) periods and plot cost against cadence, locating where human-cycle latency costs one, five, and ten percent of value.
Exercise 15.10 — Implement a particle-filter twin for a regime-switching demand state (calm/stress), compare its tracking against the Kalman twin through a regime break, and report when the Gaussian mirror is honestly wrong.
Exercise 15.11 — Meridian twin, end to end: on the AXIOM-15 module’s Meridian instance, stand up the mirror (streams, fusion, filter), certify the separation pair for the working-capital loop, run one Proposition (see book) improvement cycle, and deliver the board pack: state bands, error budget per Theorem (see book), autonomy perimeter, and the governance register.
Exercise 15.12 ★ — Develop distributionally robust twin control: replace the certainty-equivalent policy of Theorem (see book) with a Chapter 11 worst-case policy over an ambiguity ball around the posterior \(\pi_t\).

title: Chapter 14 · Artificial Intelligence for Enterprise Optimization
text:
Exercises
12 exercises, grouped A concept checks · B mathematical · C computational · D enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the advanced track. Full solutions appear in the Instructor’s Manual, Chapter 14.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 14.1 — Using Table (see book), classify six enterprise tasks as optimization, learning, or AI problems, and for each AI task name the state, action, reward, and why a single-shot predictor or static optimizer is insufficient.
Exercise 14.2 — Explain, via Theorem (see book), why placing a learned policy inside a certified optimizer’s envelope is the correct governance pattern for autonomous enterprise action, and what specifically the certificate does and does not guarantee about the AI component.


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 14.3 — Prove the Bellman optimality operator is a \(\gamma\)-contraction directly from the max-of-affine structure, and compute the exact iteration count to \(10^{-6}\) for the Example (see book) MDP; compare with the observed count.
Exercise 14.4 — Derive the policy-gradient theorem \(\nabla_\theta J = \E[\nabla_\theta \log \pol_\theta(a\mid s)\, Q^{\pol}(s,a)]\) from the definition of \(J\), and explain why the value baseline in actor–critic reduces variance without introducing bias.
Exercise 14.5 — For the Example (see book) knowledge graph, compute the personalized PageRank in closed form as \((I - d M^{\top})^{-1}(1-d)\mathbf{p}\), verify the reported top four, and show how the ranking shifts as the damping \(d\) and the affinity seed \(\mathbf{p}\) vary.
Exercise 14.6 — Prove that policy iteration terminates in at most \(\abs{\Ac}^{\abs{\Sc}}\) steps and that each non-terminal step strictly increases value at some state; exhibit the Example (see book) trajectory.


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 14.7 — Prove the Q-learning convergence theorem’s martingale-difference and bounded-variance claims in full for a finite MDP with bounded rewards, reducing the result to the stochastic-approximation theorem for sup-norm contractions; state precisely where infinite visitation is used.
Exercise 14.8 — Prove Theorem (see book)(ii) for exact branch-and-bound: the certified optimal value is independent of branching and variable-selection order, and valid inequalities derived by any (learned or classical) separator preserve the optimum.


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 14.9 — Reproduce Examples (see book) and (see book): implement value iteration and tabular Q-learning on the liquidity MDP, plot convergence, and study the sensitivity of Q-learning’s success to exploration schedule and step-size annealing (violate Robbins–Monro deliberately and report the failure).
Exercise 14.10 — Reproduce Example (see book): measure warm-start savings across a grid of prior qualities and discount factors \(\gamma\), and verify the Theorem (see book) iteration-count formula against observation.
Exercise 14.11 — Meridian autonomous treasury: using the AXIOM-14 module, build the full Definition (see book) loop for cash and liquidity management—MDP from the Chapter 5 state, RL policy with certified value-iteration baseline, knowledge- graph constraints as the verified envelope, and a governance report documenting the certificate, the envelope, and the monitoring plan for unattended operation.
Exercise 14.12 ★ — Develop distributionally robust reinforcement learning for the enterprise MDP: replace the transition law by a Chapter 11 ambiguity set per state–action, derive the robust Bellman operator, prove it remains a \(\gamma\)-contraction, and quantify on Example (see book) how the robust policy grows more conservative as the per-transition Wasserstein radius widens—connecting AIEO to the uncertainty trilogy.

title: Chapter 13 · Machine Learning for Enterprise Transformation
text:
Exercises
12 exercises, grouped A concept checks · B mathematical · C computational · D enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the advanced track. Full solutions appear in the Instructor’s Manual, Chapter 13.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 13.1 — Classify each enterprise task as supervised, unsupervised, semi-supervised, or reinforcement, and name the target and loss where applicable: churn scoring, supplier taxonomy, rare-defect detection with ten labeled cases, dynamic pricing, covenant-breach forecasting, log-anomaly triage.
Exercise 13.2 — A business unit reports a validation AUC of \(0.94\) achieved after evaluating \(400\) model variants on the same holdout.


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 13.3 — Prove the bias–variance decomposition in full, including the vanishing of both cross terms, and exhibit an enterprise estimator pair (deep tree vs stump) whose test errors order oppositely at two sample sizes.
Exercise 13.4 — Derive the ensemble variance identity of (see book) and fit its two parameters \((\rho, \sigma^{2})\) to the forest curve of Figure (see book); report the impli…
Exercise 13.5 — Show that \(k\)-means alternation monotonically decreases its objective and terminates finitely, and construct a two-cluster enterprise instance where it converges to a strictly suboptimal partition.
Exercise 13.6 — Prove the kernel form of Theorem (see book): the best rank-\(r\) reconstruction of centered data in feature space is given by the top eigenvectors of the Gram matrix, and relate retained variance to Gram eigenvalues.


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 13.7 — Extend Theorem (see book)(i) to countable classes via a weighted union bound (prior \(\pi\) on \(\Hc\), penalty \(\log(1/\pi(f))\)), and interpret the result as a generalization guarantee for regularized ERM (see book).
Exercise 13.8 — Prove that the Shapley value is the only attribution satisfying efficiency, symmetry, null, and linearity that is also given by an expectation over feature orderings; then show that dropping linearity admits alternative attributions and exhibit one.


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 13.9 — Reproduce Example (see book); then inject a leakage feature (next-quarter collections) and demonstrate the validation–deployment gap it creates under random versus time-ordered splits.
Exercise 13.10 — Reproduce Example (see book); then retrain the forecaster with an asymmetric (pinball) loss matched to \(c_u : c_o\) and report whether decision-aware training beats estimate-then-optimize regret at \(N = 150\), connecting the outcome to Theorem (see book)’s final clause.
Exercise 13.11 — Meridian learning architecture: for the working-capital GEOP of the AXIOM-13 module, design the full Definition (see book) object—estimated components, pipelines with as-of-date features, evaluation and Shapley ledgers, drift triggers—and deliver the board pack: accuracy, calibration, decision-regret history, and the two model changes the ledgers recommend.
Exercise 13.12 ★ — Develop distributionally robust learning-augmented optimization: replace the point prediction in Theorem (see book) with a Chapter 11 ambiguity set centered at the estimate with radius from the generalization bound.

title: Chapter 12 · Multi-Objective Enterprise Optimization
text:
Learning Outcome Statements
After completing this chapter, the reader should be able to:
Explain the principles of multi-objective enterprise optimization.Formulate vector-valued enterprise objective functions.Define Pareto optimality in its weak, strong, and proper variants.Interpret Pareto-efficient enterprise policies.Construct Pareto frontiers analytically and numerically.Apply weighted-sum, $\epsilon$-constraint, and Chebyshev scalarizations, knowing what each can and cannot reach.Analyze enterprise trade-offs as frontier slopes and shadow prices.Incorporate stakeholder preferences through goal programming and reference points.Implement evolutionary multi-objective algorithms with hypervolume diagnostics.Prepare enterprise models for AI-assisted optimization.

title: Chapter 12 · Multi-Objective Enterprise Optimization
text:
Exercises
12 exercises, grouped A concept checks · B mathematical · C computational · D enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the advanced track. Full solutions appear in the Instructor’s Manual, Chapter 12.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 12.1 — For each row of Table (see book), exhibit an enterprise decision where the listed tension binds, and one where the objectives are locally aligned; explain why local alignment does not license premature aggregation.
Exercise 12.2 — A consultant proposes replacing the board’s three objectives with a single weighted ESG-adjusted profit score.


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 12.3 — Prove that the set of weakly Pareto optimal points is closed when \(\Xc\) is compact and \(\Fv\) continuous, and give an example where the (strong) Pareto set is not closed.
Exercise 12.4 — Derive the closed-form frontier of Example (see book) (eliminate the budget), verify the four computed trade-off prices from its derivative, and locate where the price exceeds \(5\).
Exercise 12.5 — Show that proper Pareto optimality (bounded trade-off ratios) coincides, for convex problems, with weighted-sum optimality for strictly positive weights (Geoffrion), by adapting the separation argument of Theorem (see book)(ii).
Exercise 12.6 — Prove that the hypervolume indicator is strictly monotone: if archive \(A\) dominates archive \(B\) (every point of \(B\) dominated by some point of \(A\), not conversely) then \(H(A) > H(B)\); conclude that the true frontier uniquely maximizes hypervolume among nondominated sets.


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 12.7 — Prove that goal programming with all targets set at the utopian point and \(L_1\) aggregation returns Pareto optimal solutions, but with an attainable interior target may return dominated ones; design and justify the Pareto-restoration pass of (see book).
Exercise 12.8 — Extend Theorem (see book)(iv) to weak Pareto points: characterize exactly which weakly-but-not- strongly Pareto points are Chebyshev-reachable, and how augmentation (\(\rho > 0\)) resolves the ties.


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 12.9 — Reproduce Example (see book) with and without crowding; report hypervolume ladders, spread metrics, and the generation at which each variant first covers the knee region of Example (see book).
Exercise 12.10 — Extend Example (see book) to the full three-objective return/ESG/liquidity instance: trace the frontier by \(\epsilon\)-constraint over a liquidity ladder, exhibit the two-regime disconnection induced by the indivisible deal, and recover a dent point per regime by augmented Chebyshev.
Exercise 12.11 — Meridian board cycle: using the AXIOM-12 module’s five-channel instance (profit, resilience, sustainability, innovation, governance), run one full interactive cycle: ideal/nadir estimation, frontier gallery (evolutionary), aspiration projection, trade-off price table, and a one-page board memo recommending a stance and disclosing what it sacrifices against each channel’s attainable best.
Exercise 12.12 ★ — Develop distributionally robust MOEO: each objective replaced by its Chapter 11 worst-case expectation over a channel ambiguity set.

text:
Exercises
12 exercises, grouped A concept checks · B mathematical · C computational · D enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the advanced track. Full solutions appear in the Instructor’s Manual, Chapter 11.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 11.1 — For each source row of Table (see book), justify the recommended set-design response and exhibit an enterprise decision where the wrong family (e.
Exercise 11.2 — Explain, using the saddle point of Theorem (see book), why publishing the worst-case distribution alongside a DR decision is the correct governance disclosure, and what a board should do when that law is (a) absurd, (b) plausible.


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 11.3 — Derive Scarf’s bound of Example (see book) from the dual (see book) with \(\psi = (\xi, \xi^{2})\): exhibit the optimal quadratic majorant of \((\xi - d)^{+}\) and the two-point worst-case law, verifying Theorem (see book)’s \(k + 1 = 3\) bound is not tight here.
Exercise 11.4 — Prove the KL closed form (see book) on finite support: Lagrangian, exponential-tilt stationarity, and the one-dimensional dual; verify the Example (see book) weights.
Exercise 11.5 — Prove the \(d = 1\) concentration inequality behind Proposition (see book) using \(\Wass(P, Q) = \int\abs{F_P - F_Q}\) and Dvoretzky–Kiefer–Wolfowitz; calibrate \(c_1, c_2\) explicitly for compactly supported laws.
Exercise 11.6 — Show the worst-case expectation of Definition (see book) is a coherent risk measure, and identify the ambiguity sets representing (a) CVaR\(_{\alpha}\), (b) mean plus \(\theta \times\) standard deviation (state precisely when the latter fails coherence).


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 11.7 — Prove strong duality in Theorem (see book) directly for finite \(\Xi\) via LP duality, and exhibit a two-point \(\Xi\) instance with the Slater condition violated where a duality gap opens.
Exercise 11.8 — Prove that for \(\varepsilon > 0\) fixed and \(N \to \infty\), the Wasserstein DREO decision converges to the \(\varepsilon\)-robust decision around \(P_{\mathrm{true}}\), and combine with Proposition (see book) to obtain the full consistency diagram of data-driven DREO.


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 11.9 — Reproduce Example (see book); then implement the Wasserstein-DR newsvendor on support \([0, \infty)\) via Theorem (see book)(ii) and report whether transport-based sets repair the disappointment gap at matched regret.
Exercise 11.10 — Build the cyber layer schedule of Example (see book) across retentions \(d \in [3, 10]\) under (a) Scarf, (b) Scarf plus unimodality (Gauss-type bound; Notes), (c) fitted lognormal; plot the three curves and locate where structure assumptions matter most.
Exercise 11.11 — Meridian growth-market entry: quarterly demand history (\(N = 16\), provided in the AXIOM-11 module) with suspected survivorship bias.
Exercise 11.12 ★ — Develop dynamic DREO for the Chapter 9 controlled diffusion: define rectangular Wasserstein ambiguity per time step, derive the distributionally robust HJB equation, prove a verification theorem, and quantify on the liquidity instance how the Chapter 9 gain stiffens with the per-step radius; discuss time-consistency failures for non-rectangular sets.

Exercise 10.2 — Explain why coverage and parsimony pull against each other in granulation, and how hedges partially reconcile them; give a liquidity term set violating each principle and the resulting optimization pathology.


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 10.3 — Prove that a fuzzy set on \(\R\) is a fuzzy number (quasi-concave, u.
Exercise 10.4 — Show that the four operators of Table (see book) are ordered pointwise ({}ukasiewicz \(\le\) product \(\le\) min on \([0,1]^{2}\)), that each is a \(t\)-norm or a mean, and determine which preserve the LP structure of Lemma (see book).
Exercise 10.5 — Derive the interval-arithmetic rules for addition and scalar multiplication of fuzzy numbers via \(\alpha\)-cuts, and compute the fuzzy NPV of a two-period projec…
Exercise 10.6 — In Example (see book), derive the closed form of the frontier \(v(\lambda)\) and verify the marked coincidence $v(0.5) = $ the \(\Gamma = 1\) value; characterize instances where the fuzzy dial and the Bertsimas–Sim budget disagree at every interior point.


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 10.7 — Extend Theorem (see book) to first-order TSK systems (affine consequents) and to generalized-bell memberships, indicating exactly where the Gaussian product-closure argument must be replaced and by what.
Exercise 10.8 — Prove the possibility–probability consistency bound behind (see book): if \(\Prob\) is any probability measure with \(\Prob([\fz{U}]_{\alpha}) \ge 1 - \alpha\) for all \(\alpha\), then for every event \(E\), $(E) (E) + $ (the gap vanishing as the cut family refines), and exhibit the extremal \(\Prob\).


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 10.9 — Reproduce Example (see book); then coarsen to \(2 \times 2\) and refine to \(5 \times 5\) rules, reporting the RMSE–parsimony trade and the rule-usage histograms; prune dead rules and retrain.
Exercise 10.10 — Implement the \(\lambda\)-frontier tracer for Example (see book) with all three memberships steerable; produce the three-way sensitivity report of \(\lambda^{*}\) to core/support shifts.
Exercise 10.11 — Meridian credit underwriting: design a three-input linguistic scorecard (coverage-checked term sets), train its ANFIS on the synthetic default data of the AXIOM-10 module, assemble the NF-REO approval policy with a fuzzy loss-tolerance goal, and present the explainability dashboard a regulator would receive: rule sentences, decision surface, and the \(\lambda\)-frontier.
Exercise 10.12 ★ — Develop type-2 fuzzy NF-REO: memberships whose grades are themselves fuzzy (uncertainty about semantics).

Exercise 9.4 — Solve the Lyapunov equation of Example (see book) for \(\Sigma_{\infty}\) and size the \(\pm 2\)-sigma workforce corridors; compare with the deterministic Chapter 6 corridors.
Exercise 9.5 — Establish sharpness of the threshold in Proposition (see book): exhibit, for \(\bar\sigma^{2}\) above it, a sequence of admissible policies along which the objective diverges to \(-\infty\) slower than any quadratic bound, and conclude no quadratic verified value exists.
Exercise 9.6 — Derive the weak-error expansion \(\E[g(X^{\Delta}_T)] - \E[g(X_T)] = c_1\Delta + O(\Delta^{2})\) for Euler–Maruyama on the OU instance with quadratic \(g\) by explicit computation of both sides, identifying \(c_1\); reconcile with the measured bias ladder of Example (see book).


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 9.7 — Prove the multidimensional certainty-equivalence and drag statement: for \(dX = (AX + Bu)dt + \Sigma\,dW\) with quadratic cost, the optimal gain equals the deterministic CARE gain and the stationary value drag is \(\mathrm{tr}(\Sigma\Sigma^{\top}P)/(2\rho)\), via the matrix completion-of-squares in the stochastic HJB equation.
Exercise 9.8 — Prove the entropic–robust duality used in (see book): \(-\tfrac1\theta\log\E_{\Prob} [e^{-\theta J}] = \min_{\mathbb{Q}}\{\E_{\mathbb{Q}}[J] + \tfrac1\theta\,\mathrm{KL}(\mathbb{Q}\,\Vert\,\Prob)\}\), with the minimizing tilt identified.


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 9.9 — Reproduce Example (see book); add a control variate built from the deterministic value and report the variance-reduction factor; then add antithetic pairs and compare.
Exercise 9.10 — Solve the stationary stochastic HJB equation for Example (see book) on a grid with the diffusion term included exactly (rather than the variance-penalty proxy), using a monotone centered/upwind hybrid; compare the computed policy with the closed-form proxy and quantify the proxy’s error in gain and intercept.

Exercise 8.4 — Complete the LQ transversality check of Example (see book): show \(e^{-\rho T}V(x(T)) \to 0\) along the closed loop and along every admissible comparison path with square-integrable controls, so verification applies.
Exercise 8.5 — In Theorem (see book)’s proof, derive the mesh estimate for the piecewise-constant \(\varepsilon\)-greedy control: exhibit \(h(\varepsilon)\) such that grid maximizers on steps of length \(h\) attain the HJB maximum within \(\varepsilon\) along the trajectory.

Part C — Computational exercises

Exercise 8.8 — Prove the comparison refinement of Proposition (see book): a \(C^1\) sub solution with terminal data \(\le \Phi\) lies below \(V\); conclude the maximal-subsolution characterization in Theorem (see book) and identify where smoothness was indispensable.


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 8.9 — Implement the upwind solver for Example (see book); reproduce the feedback law and steady state, then accelerate with policy iteration on the grid and report the sweep-count ratio.
Exercise 8.10 — Reproduce the sustainability mesh ladder of Example (see book), add Richardson extrapolation, and compare the extrapolated value against \(2.9375\); verify the policy-accuracy asymmetry on the coarsest mesh.
Exercise 8.11 — Formulate Meridian’s covenant-constrained deleveraging (Example 6.
Exercise 8.12 ★ — Develop the viscosity-solution formulation for the digital instance with a hard capability floor \(c \ge \underline{c}\) (state constraint): state the constrained viscosity property on the boundary, verify it numerically for the upwind scheme, and prove convergence of the scheme using the Barles–Souganidis framework.

title: Chapter 6 · Optimal Control of Enterprise Systems
text:
Exercises
12 exercises, grouped A concept checks · B mathematical · C computational · D enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the advanced track. Full solutions appear in the Instructor’s Manual, Chapter 6.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 6.1 — Explain, using Table (see book), why maximizing \(H\) rather than \(L\) repairs the myopia of Proposition 5.
Exercise 6.2 — Read each condition of Table (see book) as a governance audit: what plan defect does each residual detect, and on which worked example would it fire?


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 6.3 — Derive the current-value costate equation \(\dot{\mathbf{m}} = \rho\mathbf{m} - \nabla_\x \tilde H\) from (see book) by the substitution \(\mathbf{m} = e^{\rho t}\lam\), and re-derive the closed form \(m(t)\) of Example (see book).
Exercise 6.4 — Verify by direct differentiation that the switching time \(t_s = 6.165\) of Example (see book) satisfies \(m(t_s) = 3\), and compute \(dt_s/dc\): how does the switch respond to the unit cost of R&D?
Exercise 6.5 — For Example (see book), derive \(g_0 = 2.4/(e^{0.8} - 1)\) from the budget equality, verify the discounted cost \(2.937\), and confirm \(\mu = 0.196\) equals the constant discounted marginal \(e^{-\rho t}c'(e^{*}(t))\).
Exercise 6.6 — In the innovation-commitment witness of Proposition (see book), complete the parameter declaration: exhibit \((\ell, c, \bar u, \rho, \delta, T)\) for which both the abstention and a commitment extremal satisfy (see book), and compute both values.


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 6.7 — Prove the variational-equation claim used in Proposition (see book) and Theorem (see book): under \(C^1\) dynamics, the flow is differentiable in the initial state with derivative solving \(\dot V = \nabla_\x f\, V\), \(V(t_0) = I\), and \(V(t)\) is invertible.
Exercise 6.8 — Extend Theorem (see book) to the terminal- manifold problem: with affine \(\mathbf{g}\) and the transversality of Theorem (see book)(ii), prove Mangasarian sufficiency for mandate-constrained instances.


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 6.9 — Reproduce Example (see book)’s shooting solve: bracket \(\lambda(0)\), locate the switch, verify \(J^{*} = 12.85\) and the pointwise maximum condition on a time grid; then re-solve by direct transcription (\(N = 48\) mesh) and reconcile the KKT multipliers with \(\lambda(t)\).
Exercise 6.10 — Implement the Riccati solve of Example (see book); reproduce \(K(0) = (0.406, 1.090)\) and the penalty \(335\), then sweep the control weight \(R \in [0.1, 2]\) and plot the tracking–effort frontier.
Exercise 6.11 — Formulate Meridian’s covenant-management block as an EOCP (leverage state, amortization dynamics, covenant band as a state constraint) and derive the unconstrai…
Exercise 6.12 ★ — Develop the constrained maximum principle for corridor- managed enterprises: state the multiplier-measure form for \(s(\x, t) \le 0\), prove the costate jump conditions at junctions in the first-order state-constraint case, and implement an arc-structured shooting method on the restructuring funnel of Example 5.

title: Chapter 5 · Dynamic Enterprise Optimization
text: Volume II, Chapter 5 · Taught in Week 10 · Lab seed 26205

title: Chapter 5 · Dynamic Enterprise Optimization
text:
Learning Outcome Statements
After completing this chapter, the reader should be able to:
Explain the distinction between static and dynamic enterprise optimization, and identify the declared mechanisms---stocks, delays, feedback, path constraints---that force the dynamic formulation.Formulate enterprise optimization problems over continuous time in the canonical Bolza form, identifying state, decision, dynamics, running and terminal value, and path requirements.Construct enterprise state trajectories as solutions of declared state-transition equations, and verify the hypotheses under which they exist, are unique, and depend continuously on data.Define time-dependent decision variables and policy functions, and distinguish open-loop schedules from feedback rules by their information structure.Interpret enterprise dynamics as optimization constraints that couple every instant's decision to every later state.Formulate dynamic objective functionals with running and terminal components, discounting, and horizon conventions.Analyze intertemporal enterprise trade-offs: investment versus harvest, smoothing versus timing, dips endured for landings.Evaluate enterprise evolution under dynamic constraints, including corridors, integral budgets, and terminal targets, and read feasibility as a property of whole trajectories.Develop complete dynamic enterprise optimization models for capital, workforce, transformation, innovation, restructuring, and sustainability planning.Prepare enterprise models for optimal control: state the formulation contract that Pontryagin's theory (Chapter 6), dynamic programming (Chapter 8), and direct transcription consume.

title: Chapter 5 · Dynamic Enterprise Optimization
text:
Exercises
12 exercises, grouped A concept checks · B mathematical · C computational · D enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the advanced track. Full solutions appear in the Instructor’s Manual, Chapter 5.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 5.1 — A planning model treats “integration fatigue” as an exogenous quarterly adjustment.
Exercise 5.2 — State the composition law (see book) in words and explain why it licenses planning \([0, T]\) in stages.


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 5.3 — Prove the integral form of Gr"onwall’s inequality used in Theorem (see book)(i): if \(\phi(t) \le a + b\int_0^t \phi(s)\, ds\) with \(b > 0\), then \(\phi(t) \le a e^{bt}\).
Exercise 5.4 — For the pipeline system of Example (see book), write \(A\) and \(B\), compute the eigenvalues and \(e^{At}\), and derive the senior-pool response to a unit hiring impulse.
Exercise 5.5 — Derive \(u^{*}(T, S) = A(T, S)/D(T)\) of Proposition (see book) in closed form (evaluate the integrals), and complete the numeric verification of strict monotonicity in \(T\) on \((0, 2.1]\) left to this exercise by the proof.
Exercise 5.6 — Construct an instance with a mixed constraint on built capability under which two admissible concentration plans blend to an inadmissible plan, completing the caveat of Theorem (see book)(iii).


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 5.7 — Prove the differential form of Gr"onwall’s inequality (if \(\dot\phi \le b(t)\phi + a(t)\) a.
Exercise 5.8 — Prove Jensen’s inequality in the integral form used by Proposition (see book)(i), with the equality characterization.


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 5.9 — Implement the capital instance with an error-controlled integrator; reproduce the four policy values of Example (see book) to three decimals and plot Figure (see book)’s trajectories.
Exercise 5.10 — Implement gradient-through-simulation for the capital instance: forward simulate, backward accumulate the discrete adjoint, and verify the gradient of \(J\) in the mesh decisions against finite differences.
Exercise 5.11 — Formulate Meridian’s covenant management dynamically: leverage state with declared amortization, covenant band as a state corridor, response schedule as the declared feedback family; audit trajectory-level admissibility across the standing scenario fan and identify the binding quarters.
Exercise 5.12 ★ — Extend the admissibility theory to state-constrained instances with viability kernels: formalize the feasibility funnel of Example (see book) as a viability kernel, develop its computation, and prove the monotone shrinkage that the simulation exhibits.

title: Chapter 4 · Nonlinear Enterprise Optimization
text: Volume II, Chapter 4 · Taught in Week 9 · Lab seed 26204

title: Chapter 4 · Nonlinear Enterprise Optimization
text:
Learning Outcome Statements
After completing this chapter, the reader should be able to:
Formulate nonlinear enterprise optimization problems in the canonical smooth form, identifying which declared blocks carry the nonlinearity and of what structural type.Distinguish convex from non-convex models precisely, and state which clauses of Chapter 3's contract each declared instance retains, weakens, or forfeits.Analyze nonlinear enterprise objective functions through gradients, Hessians, and Taylor models, and read curvature as economic structure (synergy, saturation, scale).Construct nonlinear enterprise constraint systems, including genuine nonlinear equalities, and characterize the geometry of the resulting feasible regions.Identify local and global optima, explain why the distinction now carries governance weight, and deploy the instruments (relaxation bounds, multistart, structure) that manage the gap between them.Apply first-order necessary and second-order sufficient optimality conditions, including the critical-cone curvature test.Interpret the Karush--Kuhn--Tucker conditions in the nonlinear setting: what they certify, under which constraint qualifications they are necessary, and what their multipliers price.Evaluate nonlinear enterprise trade-offs---synergy against budgets, price against volume, scale against resilience---from the geometry of the declared instance.Implement nonlinear enterprise models computationally with SQP, interior-point, and augmented Lagrangian methods, and audit candidate solutions with KKT and second-order checks.Prepare enterprise models for dynamic optimization: parametrize decisions as policies and recognize trajectory structure as the next source of nonlinearity.

title: Chapter 4 · Nonlinear Enterprise Optimization
text:
Exercises
12 exercises, grouped A concept checks · B mathematical · C computational · D enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the advanced track. Full solutions appear in the Instructor’s Manual, Chapter 4.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 4.1 — Restate the guarantee ledger of Table (see book) from memory, and for each lost guarantee name the section of this chapter that prices, bounds, or locally restores it.
Exercise 4.2 — Explain in business terms why the two local optima of Example (see book) are both honest recommendations, and why a gradient-following funding process systematically produces one of them.


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 4.3 — Compute gradient and Hessian of \(f(\x) = 5.2\ln(1+x_1) + 4.3\ln(1+x_2) + 0.3 x_1 x_2\), verify the eigenvalues \((-0.596, +0.004)\) at \((3.189, 2.811)\), and find the locus in the positive quadrant where the Hessian is exactly singular.
Exercise 4.4 — Verify every clause of the KKT system (see book) at the solution of Example (see book) using the stated numbers, and confirm LICQ by computing \(\nabla g(\mathbf{e}^{*})\).
Exercise 4.5 — In Example (see book), derive the closed form \(p^{*} = c + 1/\varepsilon\) for demand \(D_0 e^{-\varepsilon p}\) and cost \(c\), prove unimodality of profit on \([c, \infty)\), and compute the cap multiplier \(\lambda^{*}(\bar p)\) as a function of the cap for \(\bar p < p^{*}\).


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 4.6 — Prove the second-order necessary condition (SONC): if \(\x^{*}\) is a local minimizer at which ACQ holds and, for the critical direction considered, an appropriate multiplier vector exists, then \(\mathbf{d}^{\top}\nabla^2_{\x\x}\Lag\,\mathbf{d} \ge 0\) for all \(\mathbf{d} \in \mathcal{C}(\x^{*})\).
Exercise 4.7 — Prove that a finitely generated cone is closed: reduce by Carath'eodory to simplicial subcones and show each is a closed set, completing the argument of Lemma (see book).
Exercise 4.8 — Derive Gordan’s theorem from Lemma (see book): exactly one of (a) \(A\mathbf{d} < \mathbf{0}\) solvable, (b) \(A^{\top}\boldsymbol{\lambda} = \mathbf{0}\), \(\boldsymbol{\lambda} \ge \mathbf{0}\), \(\boldsymbol{\lambda} \neq \mathbf{0}\) solvable; extend to the mixed form with equality rows used in the proof of Theorem (see book)(ii).


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 4.9 — Implement the audit function of Table (see book): inputs (declared functions, candidate triple), outputs (KKT residuals, active-set report, CQ rank check, reduced-Hessian eigenvalues, verdict).
Exercise 4.10 — Run Algorithm (see book) on Example (see book) with \(20\) uniform seeds: report the basin inventory, the fraction of seeds terminating at abstention, and the concave-envelope bracket; then repeat with seeds biased below \(a = 1\) to reproduce the timid-pilot pathology.
Exercise 4.11 — Re-declare Example (see book) with the resilience architecture priced in: add the declared expected-disruption cost \(0.4\, \min(x, 100-x)^{0}\!\cdot\!\mathbb{1}\)-free smooth proxy \(0.4\,(1 - (x/100)(1-x/100)\cdot 4)^{-1}\) or a declared alternative, re-solve, and report where the concentration verdict reverses as the resilience weight grows.
Exercise 4.12 ★ — Develop second-order theory without strict complementarity: state and prove the strong second-order sufficient condition and Robinson’s strong regularity for the NEO class, and characterize the enterprise instances (zero-priced binding covenants) where the distinction is operative.

title: Chapter 3 · Convex Enterprise Optimization
text: Volume II, Chapter 3 · Taught in Week 9 · Lab seed 26203

title: Chapter 3 · Convex Enterprise Optimization
text:
Learning Outcome Statements
After completing this chapter, the reader should be able to:
Define convex sets and convex functions precisely, and verify convexity of declared enterprise objects from first principles and from the preservation calculus.Distinguish convex from non-convex enterprise optimization problems, and state exactly which guarantees are gained or lost at that boundary.Formulate convex enterprise optimization models in the canonical form $\min f$ subject to convex inequalities and affine equalities, starting from a declared GEOP instance.Analyze convex feasible regions: verify convexity blockwise, exploit closure under intersection, and read the geometry of binding constraints.Apply first-order and second-order convexity conditions to test declared objectives and constraints, with and without differentiability.Explain why local optimality implies global optimality in convex problems, and what this promise is worth in enterprise governance.Interpret Lagrangian duality within enterprise optimization: dual variables as shadow prices, weak duality as an audit bound, and strong duality as a certificate of optimality.Analyze convex enterprise resource allocation problems and derive the equal-marginal-value principle from the KKT conditions.Implement convex enterprise models computationally, using disciplined convex programming and modern solvers, and audit the returned certificates.Prepare enterprise optimization models for nonlinear generalization: identify which convex structures survive locally and which guarantees must be renegotiated.

title: Chapter 3 · Convex Enterprise Optimization
text:
Exercises
13 exercises, grouped A concept checks · B mathematical · C computational · D enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the advanced track. Full solutions appear in the Instructor’s Manual, Chapter 3.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 3.1 — State the four clauses of the convex contract of Section (see book) and, for each, name one enterprise governance question it retires.
Exercise 3.2 — The equal-marginal principle appeared three times (Examples (see book), (see book), (see book)).


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 3.3 — Prove the first-order characterization of Table (see book): differentiable \(f\) is convex on open convex \(C\) iff the tangent-underestimation inequality (see book) holds.
Exercise 3.4 — Prove the second-order characterization of Table (see book): twice-differentiable \(f\) is convex on open convex \(C\) iff \(\nabla^2 f \succeq 0\) on \(C\), and \(f\) is \(m\)-strongly convex iff \(\nabla^2 f \succeq mI\).
Exercise 3.5 — Prove the monotone composition rule (\(h\) convex nondecreasing, \(g\) convex \(\Rightarrow h \circ g\) convex) and exhibit convex \(h, g\) with \(h \circ g\) non-convex when monotonicity fails.
Exercise 3.6 — Prove that sublevel sets of convex functions are convex, and that the function \(f(\x) = \norm{\x}_2 / (1 + \norm{\x}_2)\) has all sublevel sets convex without being convex (quasiconvexity).
Exercise 3.7 — Establish the properties of Euclidean projection onto a nonempty closed convex set \(\feas\): existence, uniqueness, the variational characterization \((\x - P_{\feas}(\x))^{\top}(\mathbf{y} - P_{\feas}(\x)) \le 0\) for all \(\mathbf{y} \in \feas\), and nonexpansiveness \(\norm{P_{\feas}(\x) - P_{\feas}(\mathbf{y})} \le \norm{\x - \mathbf{y}}\).
Exercise 3.8 — Complete the coercivity bootstrap in the proof of Proposition (see book)(i): show directly from (see book) with a fixed anchor that \(f(\x) \to \infty\) as \(\norm{\x} \to \infty\) on \(\feas\), and conclude boundedness of sublevel sets.
Exercise 3.9 — Verify the convex-set fact used in Theorem (see book), Step 2: if \(C\) is convex and \(\x_0 \in \partial C\), then \(\x_0\) is not an interior point of \(\operatorname{cl} C\).


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 3.10 — Prove the subgradient existence claim of Section (see book): a convex \(f : \R^n \to \R\) has \(\partial f(\x) \neq \emptyset\) at every point.


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 3.11 — Declare Example (see book) in CVXPY, confirm DCP verification passes, solve, and reconcile the returned budget multiplier with \(\lambda^{*} = 1.0\) to solver tolerance.
Exercise 3.12 — Rewrite Meridian’s covenant requirement (interest cover within a declared band, standing price $0.9 a CEO block, and use (see book) to bound the value impact of…
Exercise 3.13 ★ — Study the statistical stability of shadow prices: if declared data are estimated with sampling error, derive distributional statements for \(\boldsymbol{\lambda}^{*}\) under strong convexity and a stable binding pattern, and connect to the resolve-threshold policy of Section (see book).

After completing this chapter, the reader should be able to:
Formulate the General Enterprise Optimization Problem in its canonical five-block form, discrete- and continuous-time.Distinguish objective functions from enterprise performance measures, and state what promotion from measure to objective adds and obliges.Identify enterprise decision variables and state variables, and keep the chosen/induced boundary exact.Construct enterprise constraint systems across the five mathematical forms and six architectural provenances.Differentiate equality and inequality constraints, and path, boundary, and decision-set constraints, with the multiplier previews each carries.Define enterprise feasibility for policies and trajectories, and certify it rather than assume it.Interpret enterprise solution spaces: feasible sets, optimal sets, and the value order that ranks only inside feasibility.Explain enterprise optimization architectures: the five-layer computational stack from declaration to implementation.Recognize the classes of enterprise optimization problems and place classical formulations as GEOP special cases.Prepare GEOP formulations for computational solution: standard forms, scaling, sparsity, and export.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 2.1 — Name the five constraint blocks of the canonical GEOP and give one Meridian instance of each, citing the block’s architectural provenance.
Exercise 2.2 — Explain why the Bolza/Mayer/Lagrange choice is representational while the \(L\)-versus-\(\Phi_T\) content split is governance, citing Theorem (see book) and the window doctrine.


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 2.3 — Write Example (see book)’s instance fully in the notation of Table (see book): every block, every symbol bound, empty blocks declared empty.
Exercise 2.4 — Carry out Mayer-to-Lagrange for \(\Phi_T(\x) = \mathbf{c}^{\top} \x\) on linear dynamics: compute the induced running term \(\ell_k\) explicitly and identify the policy-independent constant.
Exercise 2.5 — Show that exponential discounting is a declared weight inside \(L\) (define \(L_k = \beta^k \ell(\x_k, \uc_k)\)), and that re-expressing it as an augmented-state multiplicative factor yields the same optimal set; where does Theorem (see book)(iii) enter?
Exercise 2.6 — Show that rescaling constraint \(g_i \mapsto \gamma_i g_i\) (\(\gamma_i > 0\)) and variables \(\uc \mapsto \mathbf{D}\uc\) (diagonal, positive) preserves feasible and optimal sets; identify what the rescaling changes (conditioning, multiplier units) and record the multiplier transformation rule.


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 2.7 — Prove Proposition (see book)’s open-loop clause in detail: the constant-rule subclass is a declared policy class, its induced problem has the same data as the sequence-decision problem, and feasible/optimal correspondences are bijections.
Exercise 2.8 — Prove that minimal infeasible subsystems need not be unique: construct an instance with two distinct minimal subsystems and show a repair relaxing a constraint in their intersection fixes both, while one outside some subsystem fixes neither.


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 2.9 — (With AXIOM-02 or the Chapter 2 notebook.
Exercise 2.10 — (With AXIOM-02 or the Chapter 2 notebook.
Exercise 2.11 — Write one live decision of an enterprise you know in the full five-block template, empty blocks declared empty, every symbol of Table (see book) bound or explicitly unused.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 1.1 — State the forward/inverse distinction between enterprise analysis and enterprise optimization in two sentences, and give one board question that each answers and the other cannot.
Exercise 1.2 — Place each of the following in Table (see book)’s cells, justifying in one sentence: annual budget allocation at declared returns; a five-year ramp under demand scenarios; a plant-closure selection this quarter; a pricing policy responding to observed demand.


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 1.3 — Encode in the canonical form (see book): maximize two-program value \(F_1(a_1) + F_2(a_2)\) subject to budget \(a_1 + a_2 \le B\), a floor \(F_2(a_2) \ge \phi\), and nonnegativity.
Exercise 1.4 — Give a one-line counterexample to existence for each dropped hypothesis of Theorem (see book)(iii): (a) unbounded feasible set; (b) open feasible set; (c) discontinuous objective.
Exercise 1.5 — Derive the equal-marginal-value condition of Example (see book) for two funded programs by a feasible-perturbation argument: if \(F_1'(a_1) > F_2'(a_2)\) with \(a_2 > 0\), construct an improving feasible transfer, contradiction.
Exercise 1.6 — Formalize the static/dynamic boundary: show that a \(K\)-epoch open-loop problem is a static problem in the lifted variable \(\uc = (\uc_0, …, \uc_{K-1})\), and identify exactly what feature (state feedback in the policy class) breaks the lifting.


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 1.7 — Prove Theorem (see book)(iii) in detail for a problem with two constraints: show that both enumeration orders and all sign conventions yield the same feasible set and the same optimal set, and identify the algebraic properties of conjunction and sub-level sets used.
Exercise 1.8 — Prove that a product of finitely many compact blocks is compact without invoking general Tychonoff: sequential compactness and a diagonal subsequence over the blocks suffice.


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 1.9 — (With AXIOM-01 or the Chapter 1 notebook.
Exercise 1.10 — (With AXIOM-01 or the Chapter 1 notebook.
Exercise 1.11 — Write the two-page memo introducing enterprise optimization to that enterprise’s executive committee: the forward/inverse distinction, the three load-bearing words, the gates, and the first ninety days—no method names, only the discipline.
Exercise 1.12 ★ —  Formalize a partial order on policy classes by attainable value across a family of declared instances, and investigate when the value increments along a nested policy-class ladder (\(\Pol \subseteq \Pol' \Rightarrow \sup_{\Pol} \J \le \sup_{\Pol'} \J\)) admit uniform lower bounds—a first step toward pricing modeling restrictions.

Explain the complete Dynamic Corporate Transformation framework as one deductive structure.Describe the relationships among the six foundational architectures and the contracts that bind them.Summarize the General Enterprise Optimization Problem and its place as the volume's culmination.Identify the framework's unresolved theoretical questions and state them precisely.Explain the transition from enterprise architecture to enterprise optimization as a certified interface.Distinguish the scope of Volume I from that of Volume II and route questions to the correct volume.Identify research opportunities and formulate them as extensions of declared framework components.Describe the computational roadmap for the framework, from laboratory to digital twin.Understand the intended long-term evolution of the discipline, including its AI-assisted and autonomous horizons.Appreciate the interdisciplinary significance of Dynamic Corporate Transformation and locate it among its parent fields.

title: Chapter 16 · Synthesis, Future Directions, and Transition to Volume II
text:
Exercises
19 exercises, grouped A concept checks · B mathematical · C computational · D enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the advanced track. Full solutions appear in the Instructor’s Manual, Chapter 16.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 16.1 — Close the book and reproduce the canonical GEOP display with every symbol’s provenance; then check yourself against Table (see book) and Definition (see book), and list any symbol whose defining home you missed.
Exercise 16.2 — For each of the six architectures, state in one sentence what it warrants to the rest of the framework and one honesty clause it carries (Table (see book)); identify which warrant your own organization would find hardest to sign.
Exercise 16.3 — Walk the readiness chain (R1)–(R4) for a hypothetical enterprise, inventing a plausible failure at each gate; name the failure artifact and its remediation owner in each case.
Exercise 16.4 — Summarize Proposition (see book) for two audiences in two paragraphs: one to an executive who believes AI makes the theory obsolete, one to an analyst who believes AI has no place in it.
Exercise 16.5 — Classify the following questions as Volume I or Volume II questions, citing the interface row of Table (see book) that decides each: “is our plan feasible for the horizon?


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 16.6 — Prove that the dependency relation of Table (see book) is a strict partial order and that induction along it is valid (well-foundedness on the finite set); then state precisely what property of the volume’s proofs the induction step requires, per Theorem (see book)(i).
Exercise 16.7 — Write out the model-theoretic consistency argument of Theorem (see book)(v) in full: define “requirement set,” “model,” and the inference from exhibition to consistency, and identify the exact list of Meridian verifications the proof cites.
Exercise 16.8 — Make the classification argument of Theorem (see book) rigorous for a declaration you specify: list its declared quantities, assign each to one of the five classes or a coupling, and verify no residue remains.
Exercise 16.9 ★ — Prove Theorem (see book)(ii) for one concrete substitution: replace the Chapter 12 risk-functional family by a coherent alternative satisfying the same contract, and verify, hypothesis by hypothesis, that Theorems (see book), (see book), and (see book) survive verbatim.
Exercise 16.10 — Complete the necessity half of Theorem (see book): for each gate, construct an explicit minimal enterprise instance that passes all earlier gates, fails that one, and voids a named conclusion of Theorem (see book).
Exercise 16.11 — Formalize the autonomy envelope of Definition (see book) as a constraint block of the GEOP: variables, admissible-set structure, and the multiplier interpretation of its boundaries; then state one theorem about envelopes you can prove with Volume I machinery and one you cannot, explaining what O5 lacks.


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 16.12 — (AXIOM-16) Run the GEOP formulation assistant’s readiness chain on the provided mid-market instance of Example (see book); reproduce the (R1) failure, repair it, and export the four certificates.
Exercise 16.13 — (AXIOM-16) In the digital-twin demonstration, inject a drift into one declared gain and observe the divergence tripwire; attribute the violation using the three-way taxonomy and report the localization.
Exercise 16.14 — (AXIOM-16) Use the dependency navigator to trace Theorem (see book) to its primitive ancestry; report the longest citation chain and the Part I primitive it terminates on.


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 16.15 — Draft a two-page adoption memo for an enterprise you know: current position against the four gates, the first three declaration repairs, and the artifact each quarter of the first year should produce.
Exercise 16.16 — Specify a minimum-viable digital twin for a single division per Definition (see book): the declaration subset, the data bindings with frequencies, the audit schedule, and the divergence monitor—with owners.
Exercise 16.17 — Choose one row of Table (see book) and write a one-page research statement: the problem in the framework’s vocabulary, the extension point it uses, what it inherits, and the first theorem you would attempt.
Exercise 16.18 — For O4 (inter-enterprise systems): propose the declaration of a two-enterprise ecosystem—shared skeleton components, cross-register entries, and the compatibility conditions you would require—and identify which Chapter 13 results extend unchanged and which fail.
Exercise 16.19 ★ — Formulate an original DCT research project of your own design: an open question not in Table (see book), argued to be (a) expressible at a declared extension point, (b) not resolvable with Volume I machinery, and (c) valuable to a named enterprise audience.

Learning Outcome Statements

Formulate the General Enterprise Optimization Problem in canonical form and instantiate it for a declared enterprise.Identify enterprise decision variables and distinguish decisions from controls, policies, and programs.Define enterprise objective functions and value functionals from the ledgers of Parts II--IV.Construct enterprise constraint systems across the nine declared classes and audit their consistency.Formulate multi-objective enterprise optimization problems and interpret their scalarizations.Distinguish static, dynamic, multi-period, stochastic, robust, and adaptive formulations and know when each is the honest one.Analyze feasible enterprise solution spaces, including their viability-honest cores.Interpret Pareto-optimal enterprises and efficiency frontiers as governance objects.Evaluate enterprise trade-offs through multipliers, shadow prices, and frontier slopes.Prepare enterprise models for the solution methodologies of Volume II without committing to any of them.

title: Chapter 15 · Mathematical Formulation of Enterprise Optimization
text:
Exercises
24 exercises, grouped A concept checks · B mathematical · C computational · D enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the advanced track. Full solutions appear in the Instructor’s Manual, Chapter 15.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 15.1 — For each of the three inherited frameworks criticized in Section (see book) (portfolio selection, capacity planning, valuation maximization), state precisely which blocks of (see book)–(see book) it freezes, per Theorem (see book)(ii).
Exercise 15.2 — Build the decision inventory of Table (see book) for an enterprise you know: three declared levers with owners and admissible bands, and one commonly assumed lever that fails the declaration test—say why.
Exercise 15.3 — Take one KPI and write its four declarations per Table (see book) (objective component, constraint, policy input, monitor).
Exercise 15.4 — Express the difference between the stochastic, robust, and adaptive GEOPs as three one-sentence promises to a board, and state for each the declaration object that backs the promise.
Exercise 15.5 — A consultancy proposes to “optimize first and patch feasibility after.
Exercise 15.6 — Explain, without formulas, why the holdable optimum of Theorem (see book)(iii) can be strictly worse on paper yet strictly better as a plan, and which two Chapter 14 certificates it is buying.


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 15.7 — Carry out the \(K = 1\) and static-lift reductions of Theorem (see book)(ii) and Theorem (see book)(iii) in full for a two-period instance: exhibit the lifted variables, constraints, and the identification of the dynamics’ multipliers with the costates.
Exercise 15.8 ★ — Prove the Farkas alternative used in Theorem (see book)(i) for polyhedral cones, and derive the KKT conditions (see book) from it under the Slater grade, including complementary slackness.
Exercise 15.9 — Prove Theorem (see book)(iii) (the shadow-price derivative) from the perturbed value function’s concavity, and compute the covenant multiplier in a two-dimensional capital-allocation instance with explicit data.
Exercise 15.10 — Prove both directions of Theorem (see book)(ii), including the supporting-hyperplane converse under convexity, and construct a three-point nonconvex attainable set whose middle frontier point no weighted sum finds.
Exercise 15.11 — Prove Theorem (see book)(iii) and its weak converse (maximizers of \(\varepsilon\)-constraint instances are weakly Pareto optimal; strengthen to Pareto optimal under uniqueness).
Exercise 15.12 — Prove the maximum-theorem step of Theorem (see book)(i) in full for the finite horizon: upper hemicontinuity and compact-valuedness of the feasible decision correspondence, continuity of \(V_k\), and attainment.
Exercise 15.13 ★ — For the coordination result of Theorem (see book)(ii): state and prove strong duality for the partially relaxed convex GEOP under the Slater grade, show the coordinated block optima at \(\boldsymbol{\lambda}_c^{*}\) solve the unified problem, and bound the duality gap when convexity fails, relating it to the value of coordination.
Exercise 15.14 — Prove Theorem (see book)(ii): the Bellman operator’s contraction, uniqueness of the fixed point, geometric convergence of value iteration with a priori and a posteriori bounds, and existence of an optimal stationary selection.


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 15.15 — (AXIOM-15) Assemble the capital-allocation instance of Example (see book) in the GEOP builder; run both audits; report the binding set, all multipliers, and the…
Exercise 15.16 — (AXIOM-15) Trace the growth–trough frontier by weight sweep and by \(\varepsilon\)-constraint; locate the knee and the segment the weight sweep misses; report frontier slopes at three declared points and reconcile them with the instances’ multipliers.
Exercise 15.17 — (AXIOM-15) Reproduce the turnaround comparison of Example (see book): certainty-equivalent, stochastic, and robust optima with their mutual gaps; then vary the ambiguity radius and plot the price of distrust.
Exercise 15.18 — (AXIOM-15) Run the roadmap instance of Example (see book) with and without the terminal viability and holdability constraints; report the two value gaps and exhibit the corner-trap trajectory the unconstrained window recommends.


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 15.19 — Draft the one-page “priced commitments” exhibit for a real or case enterprise: five plausibly binding constraints with owners, the multiplier each would carry, and the decision each price would inform.
Exercise 15.20 — Prepare both ESG declarations of Example (see book) for a board: the ceiling form with its internal carbon price and the objective form with its frontier knee.
Exercise 15.21 — Identify a coupling in an enterprise you know that is currently “coordinated” by negotiation.
Exercise 15.22 — Design the measurement that would estimate the value of information for one dashboard upgrade: the two policy classes, the two GEOP instances, and the operational data needed to declare them honestly (Theorem (see book)(iii)).
Exercise 15.23 — For a publicized turnaround or restructuring, reconstruct the instance qualitatively: decisions, objective, the three most plausible binding classes, and whether the observed plan shows corner-trap symptoms (per-window optimality with shrinking room).
Exercise 15.24 ★ — Design a full multi-objective mandate for an enterprise of your choice: components with owners and units (Table (see book)), the chosen device of Table (see book) with justification, the frontier exhibit the board would see, and the governance protocol for moving along the frontier.

title: Chapter 14 · Mathematical Analysis of the Unified Enterprise Transformation Architecture

title: Chapter 14 · Mathematical Analysis of the Unified Enterprise Transformation Architecture
text:
Learning Outcome Statements
After completing this chapter, the reader should be able to:
Analyze the mathematical properties of unified enterprise systems: regularity, compactness, coupling, and the assumption panel that makes analysis honest.Establish the analytical consistency conditions under which the unified object is a well-posed dynamic system.Evaluate enterprise stability: spectral tests, Lyapunov certificates, certified stability budgets, and the coupling-destabilization phenomenon.Analyze enterprise feasibility beyond the single period: feasible regions, viability kernels, and recursive feasibility.Investigate enterprise sensitivity: variational dynamics, steady-state multipliers, and conditioning near criticality.Characterize enterprise equilibria: existence, uniqueness, location in the feasible region, and their response to transformation.Prove the principal analytical results governing unified enterprise evolution, invoking Parts II--III without re-derivation.Interpret each analytical property as a governance quantity with an owner, a monitor, and a failure mode.Evaluate structural robustness: how spectra, sensitivities, and feasible regions respond to structural perturbation.Prepare unified enterprise models for optimization: the assumptions, conditions, and derivative objects the GEOP consumes.

title: Chapter 14 · Mathematical Analysis of the Unified Enterprise Transformation Architecture
text:
Exercises
19 exercises, grouped A concept checks · B mathematical · C computational · D enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the advanced track. Full solutions appear in the Instructor’s Manual, Chapter 14.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 14.1 — For each row of the assumption panel (Table (see book)), name the enterprise office that owns it, one way it fails in practice, and one theorem of this chapter that dies with it.
Exercise 14.2 — “Our model produces a forecast, so the plan exists.
Exercise 14.3 — A group CFO proposes certifying enterprise stability by collecting stability attestations from the five architecture owners.
Exercise 14.4 — Explain, without formulas, why the same number governs how fast an enterprise recovers from shocks and how violently it responds to recalibrations (Theorem (see book)(iii)), and what this implies for change management during integrations (Example (see book)).
Exercise 14.5 — Give two operational consequences of backward non-uniqueness (Theorem (see book)(iv)): one for audit design and one for dispute resolution over “what caused the current state.


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 14.6 — Prove the unified Lipschitz assembly claim of Proposition (see book): for \(f\) built from layer maps by composition and superposition, exhibit the unified constant \(L\) in terms of the layer constants, and give an example where the bound is tight.
Exercise 14.7 — Prove Theorem (see book)(ii) in full, then extend it to time-varying parameters \(\theta_k\) with \(\sum_k \norm{\theta_{k+1} - \theta_k} < \infty\), bounding the trajectory drift by the total declaration variation.
Exercise 14.8 ★ — Prove the norm construction used in Theorem (see book)(i): for any matrix \(\Jac\) and any \(r > \rho(\Jac)\) there exists a vector norm whose induced matrix norm satisfies \(\norm{\Jac} \le r\).
Exercise 14.9 — Prove the block bound \(\rho(\Jac) \le \rho(\widehat{\mathbf{M}})\) of Theorem (see book)(iii), and construct a two-layer system where the diagonal blocks have radius \(0.5\), the block small-gain test fails, and the unified system is unstable.
Exercise 14.10 — Derive the steady-state multiplier \(\mathbf{S}_{\infty} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{A})^{-1}\mathbf{B}\) three ways: as the fixed point of (see book), by the implicit function theorem on \(\x^{*} = f(\x^{*};\theta)\), and by summing the walk expansion; verify the three agree on a \(2 \times 2\) example with one cross-layer channel.
Exercise 14.11 — Prove the Perron-direction comparison of Theorem (see book)(iii), and show by example that for non-normal \(\mathbf{A}\) the transient sensitivity \(\norm{\mathbf{S}_k}\) can exceed the steady-state \(\norm{\mathbf{S}_{\infty}}\) before settling.
Exercise 14.12 ★ — Prove that the viability iteration of Theorem (see book)(iii) converges to the kernel under A1–A2 with a compact admissible decision set, including the finite-intersection selection argument, and exhibit a system where \(V_j \ne V_{j+1}\) for all \(j\) (the kernel is reached only in the limit).
Exercise 14.13 — Prove the discrete invariance principle (Theorem (see book)(ii)) in full from the limit-set properties of trajectories under continuous maps on compact sets, and derive clause (i)’s global convergence as a corollary with \(V(\x) = \norm{\x - \x^{*}}\) in the contraction norm.


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 14.14 — (AXIOM-14) On the Meridian stability bench, find the single-block gain increase with the largest destabilizing effect per unit of gain (the spectral sensitivity of \(\rho\) to blocks), compare with the interaction-matrix answer of Exercise (see book), and explain any difference.
Exercise 14.15 — (AXIOM-14) Run the viability iteration on the restructuring corridor of Example (see book); report the emptying horizon, then implement both repairs and report the reopened kernels’ volumes and the repairs’ ledger costs.
Exercise 14.16 — (AXIOM-14) Continue the equilibrium \(\x^{*}(\theta)\) along the digital program’s schedule (Example (see book)); verify \(\Delta\x^{*} \approx \mathbf{S}_{\infty}\Delta\theta\) for small steps, locate where the linear prediction degrades, and relate the degradation to the Hessian flag of Theorem (see book)(v).


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 14.17 — Design the gain audit of Theorem (see book)(ii) for an enterprise you know: data sources for estimating three Jacobian blocks, the comparison procedure against declared ceilings, the escalation path for a violation, and the localization argument you would use.
Exercise 14.18 — For a publicized acquisition in your industry, reconstruct Example (see book)’s panel qualitatively: which blocks of the combined gain matrix were new, what the integration plan’s milestones were in gain-reduction terms, and one observed symptom consistent with a collapsed spectral gap.
Exercise 14.19 — Set up the monotone bracketing of Theorem (see book)(iii) for a planning cycle: specify conservative and optimistic declarations, state the order-preservation check on the Jacobian sign pattern, identify one coupling in your enterprise that would break it, and describe what the twin books certify while it holds.

title: Chapter 13 · Unified Enterprise Transformation Architecture
text:
Learning Outcome Statements
After completing this chapter, the reader should be able to:
Explain why enterprise transformation cannot be understood from any single architecture, and why unification is a mathematical necessity rather than a presentational convenience.Integrate the five enterprise architectures mathematically on their shared skeleton, and state exactly what the integration adds and what it leaves untouched.Construct Unified Enterprise Transformation Architectures for declared enterprises: skeleton, layers, couplings, declarations.Analyze interactions among architectures through the layered graph and the enterprise interaction matrix.Evaluate architectural consistency across layers, including the cross-layer cycles that defeat local audits.Model enterprise evolution in UETA: one state equation, five synchronized ledgers.Analyze architectural dependencies: who reads what, who writes what, and along which certified channels.Represent enterprise transformation holistically: a single program evaluated simultaneously in state, operator, capital, performance, and risk terms.Prepare unified enterprise models for mathematical analysis (Chapter 14) and optimization (Chapter 15).Understand the theoretical foundation that UETA provides for the General Enterprise Optimization Problem.

title: Chapter 13 · Unified Enterprise Transformation Architecture
text:
Exercises
19 exercises, grouped A concept checks · B mathematical · C computational · D enterprise applications. Starred (★) exercises are on the advanced track. Full solutions appear in the Instructor’s Manual, Chapter 13.

Part A — Concept checks


Exercise 13.1 — State the three integration principles of Section (see book) in your own words, and for each, name the theorem clause of this chapter that makes it exact.
Exercise 13.2 — “Our transformation office evaluates programs on NPV; capital and risk sign off separately.
Exercise 13.3 — A divisional CFO objects that unification will “override our capital model.
Exercise 13.4 — For each zero block a governance team might declare in \(\mathbf{M}\) (say \(\AR \to \AT\): risk never restricts operator menus), state what the declaration certifies, what would void it, and who should be accountable for its honesty (Theorem (see book)(iii)).
Exercise 13.5 — Explain, without formulas, why an enterprise that funds transformation from its stocks, manages to a plan corridor, and declares funded buffers necessarily has a constraint cycle that bilateral audits cannot certify (Theorem (see book)(iii)), and give the double-counted quantity in the canonical failure.


Part B — Mathematical exercises


Exercise 13.6 — Write out the trivial unification of Proposition (see book) for a two-component enterprise (operators, capital map, one performance dimension, one risk functional, \(\mathcal{K} = \varnothing\)), verify (C1)–(C5) explicitly, and identify the first question that forces a nonempty \(\mathcal{K}\).
Exercise 13.7 — Complete the separating constructions of Theorem (see book)(iv): for the \(\AP\) and \(\AC\) cases, give explicit numerical instances (two declarations each) and the question whose answer separates them.
Exercise 13.8 ★ — Formalize integration as a universal construction: show that \(\AS \boxplus L\) satisfies a pushout-style universal property over the skeleton (any object receiving compatible maps from the layers factors through the UETA uniquely), and derive Theorem (see book)(i) from it.
Exercise 13.9 — Two divisional UETAs share a treasury component but declare its funding-capacity indicator under different measurement contracts.
Exercise 13.10 — Prove the principal-submatrix inequality used in Theorem (see book)(ii) (for nonnegative \(\mathbf{M}\), \(\rho(\mathbf{M}_{\ell\ell}) \le \rho(\mathbf{M})\)), then generalize the gap construction: for any \(\varepsilon > 0\), build \(\mathbf{M}\) with all diagonal radii \(\le \varepsilon\) and \(\rho(\mathbf{M}) \ge 1\).
Exercise 13.11 — For the Meridian layered graph of Example (see book), prove that layered distances weakly exceed skeleton distances between paired nodes, and characterize exactly when the inequality is strict (as in the covenant’s \(3\) versus \(2\)).
Exercise 13.12 — Prove the deterministic case of Theorem (see book)(ii) directly (point-mass kernel), then show by a two-point example with a strictly convex loss functional that replacing the law by its mean breaks the square, quantifying the gap via Jensen.
Exercise 13.13 ★ — Make Theorem (see book)(v) sharp: construct a UETA in which an invariant violation is consistent with errors in two different layers within one layered SCC, and show no observation of readouts outside the SCC can distinguish them— then give the minimal additional probe that can.


Part C — Computational exercises


Exercise 13.14 — (AXIOM-13) Build the Meridian interaction matrix from Table (see book)’s standing gains, compute \(\rho(\mathbf{M})\) and its block attribution, and find the smallest single-block gain increase that crosses the cascade threshold.
Exercise 13.15 — (AXIOM-13) The consistency auditor’s preloaded instance: verify computationally that each pairwise audit passes and the joint feasibility check fails; then repair the instance three ways (relax the buffer, the corridor, the funding) and compare the repairs’ costs in the performance ledger.
Exercise 13.16 — (AXIOM-13) Seed the digital program at \((\mathrm{DIG}, T)\) and a demand shock at \((\mathrm{MKT}, S)\) in the same quarter; simulate the layered wavefronts and produce the ledger-clock table: first admissible quarter of effect for every (component, layer) readout.


Part D — Enterprise applications


Exercise 13.17 — Take an enterprise you know and draft its coupling register: at least one channel per class of Table (see book), each with carrier, direction, and a defensible gain estimate.
Exercise 13.18 — For the bank of Example (see book), write the three declarations that read regulatory capital (stock, buffer, denominator) as one skeleton quantity under (C2)–(C3), and construct a concrete pairwise-consistent, jointly-infeasible instance among the three functions.
Exercise 13.19 — Reconstruct Example (see book)’s three separated verdicts as constrained versions of the unified problem (Proposition (see book)(ii)): state exactly what each office holds fixed, exhibit the active coupling its verdict ignores, and rank the three separation gaps using the standing Meridian numbers.
